skill.name: cocoindex
skill.description: Toolkit for building and operating CocoIndex data transformation flows for AI indexing, search, embeddings, and knowledge graph workflows.
skill.summary: Build CocoIndex flows for AI data indexing, search, and incremental pipeline operations.
skill.author: Joseph OBrien

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives caused by Markdown code examples, CocoIndex API method names, and expected CLI documentation. The main confirmed risk is credential-handling guidance that tells the assistant to ask users for API key values when environment variables are missing. No prompt injection attempt, credential exfiltration endpoint, obfuscated payload, or malicious intent was found in the reviewed files.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe Credential Collection Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to ask users for API key values when provider environment variables are missing. This is legitimate setup context for CocoIndex, but it can cause users to paste secrets into chat instead of storing them directly in their environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited instructions explicitly discuss missing API keys and show API key environment variables. The risk is credential exposure through user interaction, not confirmed exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Flow Management Commands
medium_findings.description: The documentation includes commands for dropping flow resources and recreating schemas. These are normal CocoIndex operations, but they can delete persistent backend data if a user runs them against the wrong environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly documented as flow lifecycle operations and one section warns that drop is destructive. The risk depends on user environment and confirmation behavior.

medium_findings.title: External API Enrichment Template Uses Bearer Tokens
medium_findings.description: A custom function pattern sends requests to a configured external API with a bearer token. This is a legitimate enrichment pattern, but users should review endpoints and avoid sending sensitive source data to untrusted services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code is a template and the endpoint is user supplied, so there is no fixed exfiltration target. It still demonstrates outbound network access with authorization headers.

low_findings.title: CocoIndex eval Method Misclassified as Dynamic Code Execution
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged calls to text_to_embedding.eval, but these examples evaluate a CocoIndex transform flow on input text. They are not Python eval calls and do not execute arbitrary code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The semantic context shows a library method named eval used for query embedding computation. No evidence found of language-level eval or user-controlled code execution.

low_findings.title: Markdown CLI Examples Misclassified as Shell Backtick Execution
low_findings.description: Many external command findings come from Markdown inline code and fenced examples for CocoIndex CLI usage. They describe expected user-run commands rather than hidden command execution by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are documentation and the commands are visible operational examples. No evidence found of Ruby backticks, subprocess wrappers, or concealed command invocation.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs and Localhost Endpoints Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to product documentation links and local service examples. They do not identify a suspicious collection endpoint or automatic network request made by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are either official documentation links or localhost examples. No evidence found of encoded payload delivery or credential exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Labels Are Scanner Artifacts
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance labels appear to match ordinary Markdown content, status text, or diagnostic examples. No cryptographic implementation or system enumeration routine was found in the cited context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are descriptions or best-practice text, not crypto calls or reconnaissance code. No evidence found of weak algorithms such as MD5 or SHA1 in executable logic.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Prompts and Environment File Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill combines API key prompts, dotenv examples, and environment file access. This combination is expected for AI pipeline setup, but it needs user-facing caution around secret handling.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and repeated in setup guidance. It is not malicious by itself, but it can lead to accidental secret disclosure.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive Drop and Recreate Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents drop operations that remove persistent backends and suggests drop-and-recreate steps for schema mismatch troubleshooting.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The destructive nature is documented, but users could still run these examples in production. This requires caution rather than blocking.

content.value_statement: AI indexing pipelines are hard to design across sources, transforms, and targets. This skill guides CocoIndex flow creation, operation, and troubleshooting for search, embeddings, and knowledge graphs.
actual_capabilities: Designs CocoIndex flow structures for sources, transformations, collectors, and export targets.
Explains patterns for embeddings, vector databases, knowledge graphs, and live updates.
Guides creation of standalone and spec/executor custom transformation functions.
Documents CLI workflows for setup, update, evaluation, server mode, and flow inspection.
Shows Python API patterns for programmatic updates, live updaters, and query handlers.
Provides troubleshooting guidance for flow targets, database connections, schema changes, and memory limits.
limitations: It is documentation-focused and does not install CocoIndex or provision infrastructure by itself.
Users must supply valid database connections, target services, and model provider credentials.
Examples require review before production use, especially drop commands and external API calls.
It does not verify current CocoIndex package behavior against live upstream documentation.

use_cases.title: Create a Vector Search Pipeline
use_cases.description: Build a flow that reads documents, chunks text, creates embeddings, and exports searchable vectors to a target database.

use_cases.title: Extract Knowledge Graph Data
use_cases.description: Use LLM extraction patterns to convert source documents into graph nodes and relationships for Neo4j or similar targets.

use_cases.title: Operate and Troubleshoot Flows
use_cases.description: Run setup, update, evaluate, server, and diagnostic workflows while resolving schema or connection problems.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a CocoIndex flow for local markdown files. I want chunks, embeddings, and a vector search target.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Custom Function
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a CocoIndex custom function plan that cleans document text, extracts metadata, and keeps return types compatible with target schemas.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Knowledge Graph Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a CocoIndex flow that extracts entities and relationships from product documents and exports them to a graph database.

prompt_templates.title: Review an Operational Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my CocoIndex setup, update, evaluate, and live update workflow. Identify unsafe commands, missing environment variables, and deployment risks.

output_examples.input: I need to index product descriptions for semantic search.
output_examples.output: A flow design with a local or database source, text parsing, chunking, embedding generation, collection fields, and a vector database export plan.

output_examples.input: My flow fails after I changed the schema.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting checklist covering flow inspection, setup refresh, evaluation output, target schema review, and cautions before destructive cleanup.

output_examples.input: I want a custom function that calls an enrichment API.
output_examples.output: A spec/executor design with configuration fields, token handling guidance, caching considerations, error handling, and data exposure warnings.

best_practices: Use environment variables or secret managers for credentials, and avoid pasting real API keys into chat.
Run evaluate workflows before update or drop operations, especially against shared or production databases.
Keep flow definitions, custom functions, query handlers, and operational scripts separated for easier review.
anti_patterns: Do not run drop commands until you understand which persistent resources will be removed.
Do not hardcode production credentials, API keys, or database URLs in generated flow files.
Do not send sensitive source data to external enrichment APIs without endpoint and data policy review.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps users design, implement, operate, and troubleshoot CocoIndex data transformation flows for AI indexing workloads.

faq.question: Does it create a full project automatically?
faq.answer: It provides patterns and guidance. The user still needs a Python project, dependencies, services, and credentials.

faq.question: Which targets does it discuss?
faq.answer: It covers targets such as Postgres with pgvector, Qdrant, LanceDB, Neo4j, and Kuzu through examples and references.

faq.question: Can it help with custom functions?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains standalone functions, spec and executor patterns, caching, type annotations, and external API integrations.

faq.question: What security caution matters most?
faq.answer: Do not paste real API keys into chat. Configure credentials directly in environment files or a secret manager.

faq.question: Is it safe for production operations?
faq.answer: Use caution. Review setup, update, and drop commands before running them against production data stores.
